text.title: llm-observability-stack-2026 Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for llm-observability-stack-2026. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: New production LLM deployments MUST select an observability primary (Langfuse or Helicone) based on the immediate need: if cost tracking and semantic caching is the blocker, start with Helicone (15-minute setup); if prompt versioning and detailed trace analysis is the priority, start with Langfuse (2-4 hours). Repositories MUST NOT attempt to integrate both simultaneously on first deployment — the mental model collision creates implementation debt.
rule.rationale: Each platform has different architectural assumptions (Helicone as a proxy intercepts all requests; Langfuse as an SDK instruments specific call sites). Trying to run both in parallel creates double-accounting, conflicting trace IDs, and deployment complexity. Pick the primary tool that unblocks your highest-priority metric (cost or trace quality), validate the integration, then add secondary tools once the primary is stable.

rule.statement: Every production LLM system MUST track and surface these four metric categories: (1) Cost: token usage, cost per conversation, cache hit rate. (2) Quality: relevance scores, hallucination rate, user feedback. (3) Performance: latency P50/P95/P99, time-to-first-token (TTFT), throughput. (4) Reliability: error rate, retry rate, fallback triggers. Agent-specific pipelines MUST also track tool call success rate, reasoning steps per turn, and iteration count. Dashboards MUST surface these metrics with 24h rolling average and week-over-week comparison; alerts MUST trigger on >20% deviation from baseline.
rule.rationale: These four categories cover the full lifecycle: cost prevents budget shocks, quality ensures the system is solving the user problem, performance ensures the UX is acceptable, and reliability catches degradation before it reaches users. P95/P99 latency matter far more than average because tail latencies determine user perception of responsiveness. Tool call success and reasoning steps are irreducible for multi-step agents because they surface where the agent is spending computational budget and failing.

rule.statement: Observability instrumentation MUST follow the architecture: Application → Observability SDK/Proxy → (Langfuse/Helicone/Phoenix backend) → (Aggregation & Alert Engine). Trace IDs MUST be unique per request and propagated across all subagent and tool calls so a single request can be tracked end-to-end. Custom metadata (user_id, session_id, feature_name) MUST be attached to every trace at the top level; this enables cost attribution and per-feature quality analysis. SDK decorators (Langfuse @observe) are preferred for low-latency paths (<100ms SLA); proxy-based tools (Helicone) are acceptable for standard latency budgets (>500ms).
rule.rationale: Trace ID propagation is foundational: without it, a multi-step agent interaction appears as disconnected traces, and you lose visibility into cross-step costs and failures. Custom metadata at the top-level trace enables post-hoc analysis (e.g., "which features are burning budget?", "did quality drop for a specific user segment?") that SDK/proxy logging alone cannot provide. SDK vs. proxy choice is a latency trade-off: proxy adds 20-50ms round-trip, but SDK requires code changes at every call site.

rule.statement: Langfuse deployments MUST use the @observe decorator for deterministic async-safe instrumentation, attach trace metadata via langfuse_context.update_current_observation, and batch score submissions asynchronously (do not block the main response). For sensitive data (PII), MUST self-host Langfuse using the provided Docker Compose stack; MUST NOT send PII to cloud.langfuse.com without explicit data residency contract.
rule.rationale: The @observe decorator is async-safe and preserves span hierarchy; manual tracing (via trace_id threading) breaks on Python asyncio context switches and loses parent-child relationships. Asynchronous score submission is essential because quality evaluation (via LLM-as-judge) adds 200-500ms latency per call; blocking the main response on evaluation blocks the user. Self-hosting ensures PII does not leave your infrastructure and eliminates SaaS dependency for critical observability.

rule.statement: Helicone deployments MUST configure the proxy only for idempotent, deterministic requests (knowledge lookups, classification, content generation); MUST disable semantic caching for personalized or time-sensitive responses (e.g., real-time recommendations, time-aware outputs). Cache keys MUST exclude dynamic fields (current timestamp, session ID) or caching MUST be explicitly disabled per endpoint. Helicone latency overhead (20-50ms) is acceptable for API calls with >500ms SLA but MUST NOT be used for sub-100ms latency requirements.
rule.rationale: Semantic caching can serve stale responses if prompt templates use dynamic content without cache key exclusions. Personalized responses cached by default violate user expectations (e.g., a cached response from a different user). Latency overhead accumulates in tail latency (P99); for low-latency pipelines, SDK instrumentation (Langfuse) is preferred.

rule.statement: Phoenix evaluation MUST be sampled (5-10% of production traffic) to control evaluation cost, which approaches 50% of production cost at 100% sample rate. Evaluation MUST run asynchronously, not in the main request path. Embedding drift alerts MUST trigger when cosine similarity to baseline embeddings drops below 0.85 (indicating semantic shift); must not trigger on every variance. RAG retrievals MUST be evaluated with both retriever precision (did the right documents rank first?) and generation quality (was the response grounded in the retrieved docs?).
rule.rationale: Each LLM evaluation call (llm_classify, llm_score) costs tokens; at 10% sample rate on 10k daily requests, this is 1k additional evaluation calls per day. Running evaluation in-band blocks the user response; async evaluation decouples cost from latency. Drift detection without threshold tuning creates alert fatigue. RAG evaluation requires both retrieval metrics and generation metrics because a RAG system can fail at either stage.

rule.statement: Observability rollout MUST follow this sequence: (1) Deploy Helicone or Langfuse as the primary tracing platform (not both simultaneously). (2) Collect 7-14 days of baseline metrics (cost, latency, error rate). (3) Define alert thresholds based on the baseline (e.g., trigger on >20% deviation). (4) Add Phoenix evaluation for RAG systems at 5-10% sample rate. (5) Set up a weekly review cadence (SDD task or cron job) to surface metric regressions. Only after baseline is stable, add secondary tools (Braintrust for multi-agent, Portkey for fallback routing). This phased approach prevents alert fatigue and creates clear ownership for each observability layer.
rule.rationale: Deploying all observability tools simultaneously creates a firehose of data and conflicting signal. Phasing allows you to understand each platform's signal before stacking more. The weekly review cadence ensures the dashboard is not a write-only artifact; humans must regularly consume the data to notice degradation early.

rule.statement: Production LLM systems MUST version all prompts in Langfuse before any A/B test or significant prompt change. Unversioned prompts make it impossible to correlate metric changes (quality, latency, cost) to specific prompt edits after the fact; post-hoc root-cause analysis becomes guesswork.
rule.rationale: Prompt iteration is the most common lever for improving LLM output quality. Versioning enables you to say "quality dropped after we shortened the system prompt" rather than "quality dropped sometime in the past week."

rule.statement: Trace IDs MUST be unique per request and propagated across all subagent and tool calls. Custom metadata (user_id, session_id, feature_name) MUST be attached to every trace at the top level. This enables post-hoc analysis: cost attribution per feature, quality breakdown per user segment, latency analysis across geographic regions.
rule.rationale: Without trace ID propagation, multi-step agent interactions appear as disconnected traces. Without custom metadata, you cannot answer questions like "which feature is burning our token budget?" or "did quality drop for paying customers?"

rule.statement: TTFT (time to first token) MUST be tracked as a separate metric from total latency for streaming endpoints. TTFT is what users perceive as responsiveness; total latency is dominated by generation time and is less actionable.
rule.rationale: A response that takes 30s to complete but shows the first token in 500ms feels responsive; a response that completes in 10s but waits 5s before the first token feels broken. For streaming, TTFT directly impacts perceived quality.

rule.statement: Alert thresholds MUST be calibrated on production baseline data collected over 7-14 days before enabling alerts. Thresholds set on sample data or gut feeling produce alert fatigue that causes real issues to be ignored.
rule.rationale: The 20% threshold rule (alert on >20% deviation from baseline) is a starting point, but every system has different noise characteristics. Calibration on real data prevents overtuning.

rule.statement: Self-host Langfuse (via docker-compose) if the application handles customer PII. The MIT license permits self-hosting; setup is under 30 minutes. Never send PII to a SaaS observability vendor without explicit data residency and processing agreements.
rule.rationale: Self-hosting ensures PII does not leave your infrastructure and eliminates SaaS dependency for critical observability. Cost is negligible (one small VPS); value is high (compliance, data residency, independence).

rule.statement: Observability-driven agents MUST query the observability backend API daily (e.g., Langfuse API for trace aggregates), compute 7-day rolling baseline, detect metrics outside acceptable range (>20% deviation), and create SDD tasks for investigation when thresholds are breached. Agents MUST NOT directly modify thresholds or disable alerts; all alert tuning MUST go through human review. Agents MAY create draft incident reports, propose root causes (e.g., "quality dropped after prompt change on 2026-04-25"), and suggest rollbacks.
rule.rationale: Automating metric collection and threshold detection removes latency from the observability loop (humans often miss degradation until it's severe). Blocking threshold changes at the agent layer prevents cascading failures (agent disables alerts, real issue is missed). Proposal-based intervention (agent suggests rollback, human approves) balances speed with safety.
